mcaWeights.note: Weights must sum to 1.0. Adjust here to change MCA importance of each criterion.

knockouts.label: Synthetic pesticide or herbicide use
knockouts.question: Does the farm currently use any synthetic pesticides or herbicides?

criteria.title: Contextuality
criteria.description: Adapt the application of regenerative practices to the territorial, environmental, social and agronomic context of each farm, in coherence with long-term planning.

questions.text: How is pruning material managed on your farm?

options.label: Pruning is removed from fields (sold as firewood, or burned). Soil loses nutrient cycling opportunity and is labour intensive to remove.

Pruning material is left in the field as it is cut; scattered unevenly across the orchard floor. Organic matter retained but creates uneven mulch coverage; slow, inconsistent decomposition; not actively managed.
Pruning material is mulched into uniform wood chips and spread evenly across soil. Creates a consistent mulch layer for increased soil and water retention benefit.

questions.text: How do you monitor and record farm performance over time?

options.label: No systematic monitoring; farm changes such as water use, pest populations, soil health, or yields are not tracked or recorded over time.

Basic monitoring protocol developed; some key metrics (water use, yields, pest pressure, visual soil observations) are tracked with occasional records; informal note-taking; not comprehensive or regular.

Comprehensive, context-specific monitoring protocol developed and regularly updated; multiple indicators (soil health, water use, pest/disease populations, biodiversity, yields, expenses) are tracked; maintains written records or photos; conducts soil/leaf testing; uses data to guide management decisions; monitors progress toward RA goals.

questions.text: How is livestock incorporated into your farm system?

options.label: No livestock incorporated into the farm system.
Livestock utilised partly, either spatially or temporally (e.g. seasonal grazing in specific zones).
Livestock used as the primary weed suppressant across the farm.

criteria.title: Water
criteria.description: Plan, size and develop agricultural and livestock production in accordance with water availability and the geological and edaphoclimatic reality of the basin, and carry out hydrological management practices that promote responsible and non-polluting use of water and prevent the over consumption of ground water levels.

questions.text: How consciously do you manage your water use from the collective allocation?

options.label: Full water allocation provided by the collective scheme is used without consideration.
Water quantity used from collective allocation is consciously reduced based on rainfall and crop stage, and farm water efficiency is considered.

Water quantity used from collective allocation is consciously reduced and considers landscape-level water impacts; avoids capturing/storing rainfall that would deprive downstream users; recognizes that water is a shared community resource; practices water reduction that benefits both own farm and greater watershed.

criteria.title: Biodiversity
criteria.description: Implement practices that actively protect and increase both wild and cultivated/domestic biodiversity.

questions.text: How actively do you create and manage habitat for beneficial fauna and flora?

options.label: No intentional habitat creation; monoculture ground cover or bare soil; no flowering plants or native vegetation; broad-spectrum pesticides kill beneficial insects; no nesting/breeding sites provided; hostile environment for beneficial fauna and flora.

Some habitat creation emerging; plants some flowering herbs or native plants; maintains some ground cover; occasional deadwood or vegetation patches left; beginning to limit pesticide use; basic measures to encourage beneficial species but not systematic or comprehensive.

Comprehensive habitat management; intentionally plants flowering herbs, native species, and legumes year-round (spring–fall succession); maintains hedgerows and vegetation margins; provides nesting/breeding sites (deadwood, hollow stems, bare soil patches); creates water sources (bowls, troughs); regularly maintains and monitors habitat quality; actively manages habitat for specific beneficial species (above-ground pollinators, predators, parasitoids and below-ground soil fauna such as earthworms, ground beetles, springtails); understands interconnection between soil life and above-ground biodiversity.

criteria.title: Soil Management
criteria.description: There should be no tillage that turns over soil layers. Only minimal vertical tillage is permitted during transition.

questions.text: What type of tillage do you practice?

options.label: Full inversion tillage (ploughing >30 cm). Regular disturbance of soil layers.
Shallow tillage (<30 cm topsoil), reduced frequency. Some soil disturbance remains.
No tillage of any form. Soil structure left intact year-round.

criteria.title: Soil Cover
criteria.description: Keep the soil covered with plant or animal matter for as long as possible throughout the year within the hydrological availability and limitations of the basin and ensuring a positive carbon cycle limiting oxidation.

questions.text: How is soil cover managed on your farm throughout the year?

options.label: Little to no natural soil cover at any time of year; bare soil exposed; no ground cover strategy.
Soil cover present but not managed; grows unchecked; may compete with main crop for water/nutrients; farmer does not actively control cover amount or timing.

Soil cover actively managed through intentional winter and summer cover crop rotations; winter cover crops (e.g., clover, vetch) planted and allowed to establish; summer cover crops managed (rolled, cut, or grazed) to prevent competition with citrus during water-stressed season; cover crops regrow annually from established root systems; farmer maintains optimal balance between soil protection and citrus water/nutrient availability through seasonal management.

criteria.title: Crop Diversification
criteria.description: In the case of seasonal crops, apply associations and/or rotations and/or crop diversification by combining them in space and time. In the case of perennial crops, apply intercropping and/or agroforestry and/or vegetation diversification.

questions.text: How diverse is your cropping system — do you use intercropping or alley cropping between citrus rows?

options.label: Single crop variety. No deliberate diversification. No intercropping between rows.
Some alley cropping. Limited species variety.
Active intercropping or alley cropping utilised, including leguminous species. High inter-row diversity.

questions.text: What types of citrus varieties are planted on your farm?

options.label: Farm planted with conventional citrus varieties bred for high-input systems; require synthetic fertilizers and frequent irrigation; poor drought tolerance; not suited to RA conditions.
Farm contains a mix of conventional and drought-resistant varieties; gradual replanting toward water-efficient cultivars; some plants suited to RA, others still dependent on high inputs.

Farm planted primarily with drought-resistant citrus varieties or heirloom/indigenous varieties suited to RA conditions; require less water and synthetic inputs; naturally resilient to local climate and pests; compatible with low-input RA management.

criteria.title: Inputs
criteria.description: Manage the farm without synthetic chemical fertilizers or biocides (herbicides, insecticides and fungicides) or extensive use of external inputs that exceed the limitations of the basin.

questions.text: What is your use of synthetic pesticides, herbicides, and fungicides?

options.label: Conventional use of synthetic chemical fertilisers, herbicides, and pesticides.
Reduced synthetic inputs. Some organic alternatives introduced.
Only organic biocides used. No synthetic chemicals, transgenics, or GMO-derived inputs.

questions.text: How do you manage Soil Organic Matter (SOM)?

options.label: No SOM added to soil; relies on synthetic fertilizers only.
SOM is added occasionally; compost, manure, or green manure crops used inconsistently; not yet standard practice.
SOM amendments are an integral and regular practice; compost, manure, or green manure crops applied systematically as core fertility strategy.

questions.text: How do you manage micronutrients on your farm?

options.label: Only generic NPK fertiliser added to soil routinely.
Micronutrients added only to address visible deficiencies.
Specific deficiencies identified through lab tests and only those micronutrients are applied to the areas where they are required.

questions.text: What type of fertiliser do you apply?

options.label: Only synthetic fertiliser is applied.
A combination of synthetic and organic fertiliser is applied.
Only organic fertiliser used and only applied as required.

questions.text: How do you manage pests on your farm?

options.label: No biological pest management employed.
Natural traps and predators used for invasive and indigenous pests.
Natural traps and predators used only for invasive pests. An environment is created that naturally attracts beneficial insects to manage indigenous pests.

criteria.title: Plastics & Waste
criteria.description: Minimize the use of plastic and non-recyclable materials, and ensure proper recycling of those that are used.

questions.text: What type of mulching do you use on your farm?

options.label: Low-quality plastic mulching used as primary mulching form.
Farmer routinely uses a combination of plastic and organic mulching.

Plastic mulching is used only when absolutely necessary (e.g., invasive plants) and must be high-quality permeable tarpaulin that does not degrade and allows the soil to breathe and receive rainwater.

questions.text: How do you manage general plastic use and disposal on the farm?

options.label: Plastic is burned or incorporated into the soil.
Farmer limits plastic use and removes them from the farm before they degrade.
High-quality plastic only used where necessary and is sustainably disposed of before it degrades.

criteria.title: Social & Territorial Foundations
criteria.description: Promote landscape-wide cooperation and knowledge transfer, to strengthen the community and the sustainability of territories.

questions.text: How actively do you participate in knowledge exchange and learning with other farmers?

options.label: Farmer does not participate in knowledge exchange events. Receives information only through top-down channels.
Farmer participates 2–5 times per year in events or learns mainly through informal personal networks.
Farmer actively organises or participates 3–5+ times per year in farmer networks, field days, or peer-to-peer learning opportunities.

questions.text: How connected do you feel to Valencia and the local farming community?

options.label: Farmer operates as an individual with little connection or identity with Valencia or the surrounding area.
Farmer feels a connection to the region but has not translated this into active community engagement.
Farmer feels a strong connection to Valencia, invests in the community, and demonstrates social cohesion with the surrounding area.

questions.text: What is your involvement in cooperatives or agricultural networks?

options.label: Farmer has not joined any cooperative. Little access to grants, networks, or technical support.
Farmer is a member of a cooperative but invests little energy in networking opportunities.
Farmer is active in cooperatives, has good access to grants, policy connections, and local institutions.

criteria.title: Economic Viability
criteria.description: The farming practices should promote a minimal financial input by using on-farm inputs whilst the farmer should have market access to ensure their product can be sold through the shortest possible supply chain.

questions.text: Where do the majority of your farm inputs come from?

options.label: All inputs are purchased from commercial enterprises.
Some inputs are produced on-farm or purchased from other farmers.
The majority of inputs are either produced on-farm or purchased from other farmers.

questions.text: How do you sell your produce?

options.label: All produce is sold at wholesale price directly to intermediaries, without any label or certification.
Produce is sold through either a cooperative/organisation or through direct sales to consumers or businesses.
Produce is sold through cooperative/organisation or direct sales and carries official certification.
